title: Long time dynamics of 2d Euler and nonlinear inviscid damping
speaker: Hao Jia
abstract: In this talk, we will discuss some joint work with Alexandru Ionescu on the nonlinear inviscid damping near point vortex and monotone shear flows in a finite channel. We will put these results in the context of long time behavior of 2d Euler equations and indicate further important open problems in the field.

proof_stages.label: 2D Euler long-time problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the 2D Euler equation in vorticity form, emphasizes global smoothness but absence of viscosity or dispersion, and explains why existing stability or unstable-manifold results do not give generic long-time asymptotics.

proof_stages.label: Vortex symmetrization and point-vortex theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker isolates vortex axisymmetrization from numerical dynamics, recalls the still-open nonlinear problem for smooth decreasing radial vortices, and states a proved special theorem in which a smooth perturbation of a moving point vortex weakly converges to a radial profile while the point vortex stabilizes.

proof_stages.label: Monotone shear-flow damping theorem
proof_stages.summary: The analogous problem is set up for monotone shear flows on a periodic channel, with Gevrey-2 smallness, support away from the boundary, and a no-discrete-eigenvalue assumption, leading to nonlinear inviscid damping: in a transported profile the vorticity remains controlled and converges, while the velocity converges to a final shear flow.

proof_stages.label: Proof framework and sharp linear estimates
proof_stages.summary: The proof is organized around linear estimates in spaces suitable for nonlinear analysis, nonlinear coordinate changes, and a decoupling step; the linearized shear equation is analyzed through Fourier modes, sharp stream-function decay, and the Couette model showing why the precise resonant denominator and moving coordinates must be retained.

proof_stages.label: Spectral-density singularity analysis
proof_stages.summary: For general monotone shear, the linear flow is represented by a spectral density satisfying a singular PDE, and changing variables to v=b(y), w=b(y0) and then shifting variables isolates the singularity so that the spectral regularity needed for sharp decay estimates can be obtained.

proof_stages.label: Nonlinear renormalized coordinates
proof_stages.summary: The nonlinear transport is undone by passing to variables tracking the x-transport and speed, producing a transformed vorticity f satisfying a self-contained transport equation whose velocity field has quadratic-in-time decay, making uniform regularity plausible.

proof_stages.label: Imbalanced Gevrey energy bootstrap
proof_stages.summary: A time-decreasing, imbalanced Gevrey-weighted energy is introduced to absorb the large resonant elliptic factor, compensate gradual loss of regularity, and close the nonlinear damping control, with the final linear/nonlinear coupling step only briefly indicated due to time.

examples.name: Zero solution as only L2-stable information
examples.note: The zero vorticity solution is used to illustrate that an Arnold-type L2 stability statement can be true while giving essentially no asymptotic description of nearby dynamics.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker verifies the zero solution's L2 stability by invoking the energy estimate obtained by multiplying the Euler equation by omega

passes.reason: The zero Euler state is trivial and maximally invariant, and it is invoked to show L2 stability alone is not a real dynamical description.
the trivial zero Euler flow is the minimal-norm collapsed case and is invoked to show that L2 stability alone gives no dynamics

examples.name: Periodic-torus vortex simulation
examples.note: The displayed 2D Euler simulation shows small vortices being filamented by larger ones and same-sign vortices sometimes merging, motivating the choice to analyze the axisymmetrization mechanism rather than the harder merger mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker plays and analyzes the periodic-torus simulation, tracing how blobs of vorticity are filamented, absorbed, and reduced to two large vortices

examples.name: Small patch near a round vortex
examples.note: In response to a question, a small perturbing patch around a radial vortex is used to explain that radius-dependent angular velocity winds the patch into a radial profile, the same differential-transport idea later encoded in moving coordinates.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through a small patch near a round vortex, explaining differential rotation by radius and how it becomes radialized

passes.reason: The localized patch is a generic perturbation near a radial vortex, used to illustrate filamentation and axisymmetrization.

examples.name: Positive and negative point-vortex pair
examples.note: A question asks whether the point-vortex symmetrization analysis could be extended to one positive and one negative vortex, illustrating a natural but currently difficult multi-vortex extension involving separation and interaction of signs.
